tasks.title: Recurring tasks — full vertical slice with recurrence scheduler
tasks.description:
Add recurring task support — Prisma fields, GraphQL mutations, background scheduler, and frontend UI.

**1. Prisma schema** (`apps/api/prisma/schema/task.prisma`):
Add to the Task model:
```
recurrenceRule    String?   // cron expression e.g. "0 9 * * 1" (every Monday 9am)
recurrenceParentId String?  // FK to the original recurring task template
recurrenceLastCreated DateTime? // when the last instance was created
```
Add relation: `recurrenceParent Task? @relation("RecurrenceChain", fields: [recurrenceParentId], references: [taskId])` and `recurrenceChildren Task[] @relation("RecurrenceChain")`. Add index on `[recurrenceRule]` for the scheduler query. Run `npx prisma migrate dev --name add_task_recurrence` to generate migration.

**2. GraphQL schema** (`apps/api/src/graphql/typedefs/task.ts`):
Add `recurrenceRule: String`, `recurrenceParentId: ID` to the Task type. Add `recurrenceRule` as optional param to the `updateTask` mutation (set to empty string to clear). No separate mutation needed — recurrence is just a field on the task.

**3. Resolver changes** (`apps/api/src/graphql/resolvers/task.ts`):
- In `updateTask`, accept `recurrenceRule` param. Validate it with a simple cron regex: `/^(\*|\d+)(\/(\d+))?( (\*|\d+)(\/(\d+))?){4}$/` or use a small helper. Store it on the task. Log an activity entry when recurrence is set/cleared.
- Add auto-review trigger: when status changes to `in_review` AND the task has linked PRs (`pullRequests` from GitHub), call `aiReviewCode` from `aiService.ts` in fire-and-forget mode. Import `reviewCode` from `../ai/aiService.js`. Get the API key from context org. Wrap in try/catch so failures don't block the status update. Store the review result on the task or log it.

**4. Recurrence scheduler** (`apps/api/src/utils/recurrenceScheduler.ts`):
Create a new utility that runs on a 60-second `setInterval`:
```typescript
export function startRecurrenceScheduler(prisma: PrismaClient): NodeJS.Timeout {
  return setInterval(() => processRecurrence(prisma).catch(err => log.error({ err }, 'Recurrence processing failed')), 60_000);
}
export function stopRecurrenceScheduler(timer: NodeJS.Timeout): void { clearInterval(timer); }
```
`processRecurrence` queries tasks where `recurrenceRule IS NOT NULL` and checks if the current time matches the cron expression. Use a simple cron-matching function (do NOT install an npm package — write a ~30 line helper that parses 5-field cron and checks against current time). When a match fires and `recurrenceLastCreated` is null or older than 23 hours ago (debounce), create a new task copying title, description, instructions, priority, projectId, sprintId, and set `recurrenceParentId` to the template task's ID. Update `recurrenceLastCreated` on the template.

**5. Wire scheduler** (`apps/api/src/index.ts`):
Import and call `startRecurrenceScheduler(prisma)` after the server starts (next to the existing `startRetryProcessor`). Call `stopRecurrenceScheduler` in the graceful shutdown handler.

**6. Frontend** (`apps/web/src/components/TaskDetailPanel.tsx`):
Add a recurrence section below the due date field. Show a dropdown/select with common options: "None", "Daily", "Weekly (Mon)", "Weekly (Fri)", "Biweekly", "Monthly". Map selections to cron expressions. When changed, call `updateTask` with the new `recurrenceRule`. Show a small repeat icon (↻) badge next to the task title when `recurrenceRule` is set. Add `recurrenceRule` and `recurrenceParentId` to `TASK_FIELDS` in `apps/web/src/utils/taskHelpers.ts` and to the `Task` interface in `apps/web/src/types.ts`.
acceptanceCriteria: Run npx prisma generate && pnpm typecheck passes
pnpm lint passes
Migration file included in commit
updateTask accepts recurrenceRule param and stores it
Recurrence scheduler runs on 60s interval and creates child tasks when cron matches
Scheduler is wired into index.ts startup and shutdown
TaskDetailPanel shows recurrence dropdown with common presets
TASK_FIELDS includes recurrenceRule and recurrenceParentId
Auto-review triggers on status change to in_review when task has linked PRs

tasks.title: File attachments on tasks — upload endpoint, Prisma model, UI
tasks.description:
Add file attachment support for tasks — local storage with REST upload endpoint, GraphQL types, and TaskDetailPanel UI.

**1. Install multer** — `pnpm --filter api add multer && pnpm --filter api add -D @types/multer`. This handles multipart/form-data file uploads.

**2. Prisma model** (`apps/api/prisma/schema/task.prisma`):
Add a new model at the bottom of the file:
```
model Attachment {
  attachmentId String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId       String
  task         Task     @relation(fields: [taskId], references: [taskId], onDelete: Cascade)
  fileName     String
  fileKey      String   // path on disk or S3 key
  mimeType     String
  sizeBytes    Int
  uploadedById String
  uploadedBy   User     @relation("UserAttachments", fields: [uploadedById], references: [userId])
  createdAt    DateTime @default(now())
  @@index([taskId])
}
```
Add reverse relation on Task: `attachments Attachment[]`. Add reverse relation on User in `apps/api/prisma/schema/auth.prisma`: `attachments Attachment[] @relation("UserAttachments")`. Run `npx prisma migrate dev --name add_attachments`.

**3. Upload REST endpoint** (`apps/api/src/routes/upload.ts`):
Create a new Express router following the export.ts pattern. Use multer with disk storage:
```typescript
const uploadDir = path.resolve(process.cwd(), 'uploads');
fs.mkdirSync(uploadDir, { recursive: true });
const storage = multer.diskStorage({
  destination: uploadDir,
  filename: (_req, file, cb) => cb(null, `${Date.now()}-${file.originalname}`),
});
const upload = multer({ storage, limits: { fileSize: 10 * 1024 * 1024 } }); // 10MB max
```
- `POST /api/uploads/:taskId` — authenticated, upload single file. After upload, create Attachment record in DB. Return the attachment JSON.
- `DELETE /api/uploads/:attachmentId` — authenticated, verify user owns the attachment's org, delete file from disk and record from DB.
- `GET /api/uploads/:attachmentId` — serve the file (use `res.sendFile`). Verify user has access to the task's project.
- Add rate limiter: 10 uploads per minute per user.

**4. Register route** (`apps/api/src/app.ts`):
Import the upload router and register it: `app.use('/api/uploads', uploadRouter)`. Place it AFTER the auth rate limiter but BEFORE the GraphQL endpoint. The multer middleware must be registered before the express.json() body parser for multipart requests — OR register it only on the upload router (which is self-contained).

**5. GraphQL types** (`apps/api/src/graphql/typedefs/task.ts`):
Add `type Attachment { attachmentId: ID!, taskId: ID!, fileName: String!, fileKey: String!, mimeType: String!, sizeBytes: Int!, uploadedById: ID!, createdAt: String! }`. Add `attachments: [Attachment!]!` to the Task type. Add `deleteAttachment(attachmentId: ID!): Boolean!` mutation. The upload itself is REST (multipart), not GraphQL.

**6. Task resolver** (`apps/api/src/graphql/resolvers/task.ts`):
Add a field resolver for `Task.attachments` that loads from DB: `attachments: (parent) => prisma.attachment.findMany({ where: { taskId: parent.taskId } })`. Add `deleteAttachment` mutation that verifies org access and deletes the record + file.

**7. Frontend** (`apps/web/src/components/TaskDetailPanel.tsx`):
Add an "Attachments" section below the description. Show a list of attached files with name, size, and a download link (`/api/uploads/${attachmentId}`). Add a file input button ("Attach File") that POSTs to `/api/uploads/${taskId}` with FormData. After successful upload, refresh the task data. Add a delete button (x) on each attachment. Add `attachments` to `TASK_FIELDS` in `taskHelpers.ts` and to the `Task` interface in `types.ts`.

Note: This task shares TaskDetailPanel.tsx and taskHelpers.ts with task-001. Complete task-001 first, then build on those changes.
acceptanceCriteria: Run npx prisma generate && pnpm typecheck passes
Verify pnpm typecheck passes — add @types/multer or declare module in a .d.ts if needed
pnpm lint passes
Migration file included in commit
POST /api/uploads/:taskId accepts multipart file upload and creates Attachment record
GET /api/uploads/:attachmentId serves the file
DELETE /api/uploads/:attachmentId removes file and record
Upload limited to 10MB per file
TaskDetailPanel shows attachments list with upload button and delete
TASK_FIELDS includes attachments with all fields

tasks.title: Subtask-level code generation — iterate subtasks in CodePreviewModal
tasks.description:
When a task has subtasks, generate code per-subtask instead of for the entire parent task. This keeps AI output within token limits and produces better-scoped code.

**1. New GraphQL mutation** (`apps/api/src/graphql/typedefs/ai.ts`):
Add `generateCodeFromSubtask(taskId: ID!, subtaskId: ID!, styleGuide: String): CodeGeneration!`. This generates code for a single subtask, using the parent task's context (project, instructions) plus the subtask's specific title/description.

**2. Resolver** (`apps/api/src/graphql/resolvers/ai.ts`):
Add a new resolver `generateCodeFromSubtask`. Implementation:
- Load the subtask by `subtaskId`, verify it belongs to the parent `taskId`
- Load the parent task for context (instructions, project)
- Call `aiGenerateCode` with the subtask's title+description but the parent's instructions as additional context
- Prompt modification: in the userPrompt, include "This is a subtask of '{parentTitle}'. Parent instructions: {parentInstructions}. Generate code ONLY for this specific subtask: {subtaskTitle} — {subtaskDescription}"

Alternatively, reuse the existing `generateCodeFromTask` resolver but have it check for subtasks and call per-subtask. The cleaner approach is the new mutation since it gives the frontend explicit control.

**3. Frontend — CodePreviewModal changes** (`apps/web/src/components/CodePreviewModal.tsx`):
Currently the modal receives pre-generated files. Modify the flow:
- Add a new prop `subtasks?: Task[]` to CodePreviewModal
- If subtasks are provided and non-empty, show a "Generate by subtask" mode:
  - List each subtask with a "Generate" button next to it
  - When clicked, call the new `generateCodeFromSubtask` mutation for that subtask
  - Accumulate generated files across subtasks in local state
  - Show all generated files together with a label indicating which subtask produced them
  - "Create PR" sends all accumulated files at once
- If no subtasks, fall back to current behavior (single-task generation)

**4. Frontend — useAIGeneration hook** (`apps/web/src/hooks/useAIGeneration.ts`):
Add a new function `handleGenerateCodeFromSubtask(taskId: string, subtaskId: string)` that calls the new mutation. Add the GraphQL mutation string to `apps/web/src/api/queries.ts`:
```graphql
mutation GenerateCodeFromSubtask($taskId: ID!, $subtaskId: ID!, $styleGuide: String) {
  generateCodeFromSubtask(taskId: $taskId, subtaskId: $subtaskId, styleGuide: $styleGuide) {
    files { path content language description }
    summary
    estimatedTokensUsed
    delegationHint
  }
}
```

**5. Wire subtasks into CodePreviewModal** (`apps/web/src/pages/ProjectDetail.tsx`):
When opening CodePreviewModal, pass the selected task's subtasks (from `d.subtasks[task.taskId]`). The subtasks are already loaded when a task is selected via `selectTask()` in useTaskCRUD.
acceptanceCriteria: pnpm typecheck passes
pnpm lint passes
generateCodeFromSubtask mutation exists and generates code scoped to a single subtask
CodePreviewModal shows per-subtask generation UI when subtasks are available
Generated files from multiple subtasks accumulate and can be submitted as one PR
Falls back to single-task generation when no subtasks exist
New mutation query added to queries.ts

tasks.title: API docs operation descriptions from SDL comments
tasks.description:
The API docs page at `/api/docs` lists all GraphQL operations but only shows their signature — no description of what each operation does. Extract descriptions from SDL comments and display them. **1. Add SDL descriptions** (`apps/api/src/graphql/typedefs/`): GraphQL supports `"""description"""` strings above field definitions. Add descriptions to key operations across the typedefs files. You don't need to add them to ALL operations — focus on the most important ones (15-20 operations). Examples: In `typedefs/task.ts`: ```graphql """List tasks for a project. Returns paginated results with hasMore flag.""" tasks(projectId: ID!, parentTaskId: ID, limit: Int, offset: Int): TaskConnection! """Create a new task in a project. Defaults to 'todo' status.""" createTask(projectId: ID!, title: String!, status: String, taskType: String): Task! ``` In `typedefs/ai.ts`: ```graphql """Generate a task breakdown plan for a project using AI. Returns preview tasks for approval.""" previewTaskPlan(projectId: ID!, context: String): [TaskPlanPreview!]! """Generate implementation code for a task using AI. Requires task to have instructions.""" generateCodeFromTask(taskId: ID!, styleGuide: String): CodeGeneration! ``` Add descriptions to: tasks, createTask, updateTask, bulkUpdateTasks, createProject, sprints, createSprint, comments, createComment, notifications, previewTaskPlan, commitTaskPlan, generateCodeFromTask, generateTaskInstructions, reviewPullRequest, signup, login, me, projects. **2. Parse descriptions in docs route** (`apps/api/src/routes/docs.ts`): The `parseOperations` function (around line 31-38) currently extracts just operation names from the SDL text. Modify it to also capture the `"""description"""` triple-quote strings that precede each operation. Update the regex or parser to: - Look for `"""..."""` immediately before a field name in Query/Mutation blocks - Store description alongside the operation name - Return `Array<{ name: string; description?: string }>` **3. Render descriptions in HTML** (`apps/api/src/routes/docs.ts`): In the HTML template where operations are listed, render the description below each operation name in a `

` tag. If no description exists, show nothing (backwards compatible). **4. Add descriptions to schema endpoints** (`apps/api/src/routes/docs.ts`): The `/api/docs/schema.json` endpoint should include descriptions in its output. If the schema is built with graphql-yoga's `createSchema`, descriptions from SDL are automatically included in the introspection result — verify this works.
acceptanceCriteria: pnpm typecheck passes
pnpm lint passes
At least 15 operations have triple-quote SDL descriptions
GET /api/docs shows descriptions below operation signatures
Operations without descriptions render cleanly (no empty elements)
GraphiQL introspection shows the descriptions in its docs panel
tasks.reviewNotes: Merge conflicts in apps/api/src/graphql/typedefs/ai.ts and apps/api/src/graphql/typedefs/task.ts. Your branch still contains task-003 commits that have already been merged to main from a different squash. Please rebase onto main (git fetch origin && git rebase main) to resolve the conflicts, then mark as completed again.

tasks.title: Project and notification integration tests
tasks.description:
Extend integration test coverage to project and notification resolvers, following the existing patterns in `auth.integration.test.ts` and `task.integration.test.ts`.

**1. Project integration tests** (`apps/api/src/__tests__/project.integration.test.ts`):
Follow the existing test pattern — import from `setup.integration.ts`, use `setupTestDatabase()`/`cleanDatabase()`/`teardownTestDatabase()`.

- **Setup:** Create a user + org in `beforeAll`. Build a context object using `makeContext()` pattern from task.integration.test.ts.
- **createProject:** Import `projectMutations` from `apps/api/src/graphql/resolvers/project.ts`. Call `projectMutations.createProject({}, { name: 'Test Project' }, ctx)`. Verify project is created in DB with correct name, orgId. Test that non-admin users get AuthorizationError.
- **updateProject:** Create a project, update its name and description. Verify DB reflects changes. Test updating `statuses` with valid JSON array. Test that invalid statuses JSON throws ValidationError.
- **archiveProject:** Call `projectMutations.archiveProject({}, { projectId }, ctx)`. Verify `archived` flag is set. Test that archived projects still appear in queries when `includeArchived: true`.
- **project query:** Call `projectQueries.project({}, { projectId }, ctx)`. Verify it returns the correct project. Test that querying another org's project throws NotFoundError.

At least 8 test cases.

**2. Notification integration tests** (`apps/api/src/__tests__/notification.integration.test.ts`):
- **Setup:** Create user + org + project + task in `beforeAll`. Create some test notifications using `prisma.notification.create()`.
- **notifications query:** Verify returns notifications for the authenticated user. Test `unreadOnly: true` filter. Test `limit` parameter.
- **unreadNotificationCount:** Create 3 unread + 2 read notifications. Verify count is 3.
- **markNotificationRead:** Mark one notification as read. Verify `isRead` is true. Test marking another user's notification throws NotFoundError.
- **markAllNotificationsRead:** Create 5 unread notifications. Call mutation. Verify all are now read. Verify return value is 5 (count).
- **notificationPreferences:** Query default preferences. Verify 6 default notification types are returned. Update a preference (inApp: false). Verify it persists.

At least 7 test cases.

Import resolvers from their respective files:
```typescript
import { projectQueries, projectMutations } from '../graphql/resolvers/project.js';
import { notificationQueries, notificationMutations } from '../graphql/resolvers/notification.js';
```

Use `buildLoaders` from `apps/api/src/graphql/loaders.ts` to construct the context's `loaders` field.
acceptanceCriteria: pnpm typecheck passes
pnpm --filter api test passes (unit tests)
project.integration.test.ts has at least 8 test cases covering CRUD + auth + validation
notification.integration.test.ts has at least 7 test cases covering queries, mutations, preferences
Integration tests use existing setup.integration.ts helpers
Tests follow the makeContext() pattern from existing integration tests
